title: 帷幕
summary:
At Stiles Manor, a narrator addresses Blackstone, explaining that he summoned him to act as his "eyes and ears" to witness a specific sequence of events. The narrator reveals that he previously withheld the identity of "X" to facilitate a specific realization. He explains that X is not a direct murderer but a "catalyst"—a third substance that triggers a reaction between others without participating in the crime itself. Using literary parallels, the narrator compares X to Iago from *Othello*, a character who manipulates others into committing violence while remaining above suspicion, and to Cruit John from *John Fergusson*, who uses psychological suggestion to lure others into killing.

The narrator confesses that he came to Stiles because he intended to commit a murder himself to achieve a greater goal of saving the innocent, though he compares his hesitation to Hamlet. He then reveals that X is Stephen Norton. Blackstone had correctly suspected Norton due to his inconspicuous nature. The narrator describes Norton’s background as a man with a domineering mother and a history of being mocked for his aversion to blood and violence. This trauma led Norton to discover a sense of power through psychological manipulation.

Norton is identified as a sadist and an addict of "secondary violence" who uses his compassionate, listening nature to manipulate the hidden desires of others, effectively destroying their will to resist murderous impulses. The narrator notes that Norton’s crimes extend beyond five known cases; he has manipulated Ezelington, Rigs, Frieda Kre, and Margaret Ritchfield by using psychological stimuli to influence their actions and perceptions.
